on_prem.implementation_notes: Customer develops and maintains access control policies for their environment.

Meridian implements secure error handling in the LMS (no sensitive data in error messages). | See Meridian Secure Config Guide: MGDevelopmentToolsetUsers, EnvironmentStatus.IpRange
Customer manages data retention and disposal policies for LMS data. | See Meridian Secure Config Guide: GDPR_STATUS
Customer manages memory protection at the OS/infrastructure level. | See Meridian Secure Config Guide: validateRequest

Customer develops SCRM plan for their environment.
Customer implements supply chain controls for procured components.
Customer uses secure acquisition practices.
Customer assesses and reviews suppliers including Meridian.
Customer establishes notification agreements with suppliers.
Customer inspects delivered systems/components.
Customer verifies authenticity of components.

cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Customer develops and maintains access control policies. Meridian provides application security documentation for reference.

Meridian provides user provisioning, role assignment, and account lifecycle features within the LMS. Customer manages onboarding/offboarding decisions, periodic access reviews, and IdP integration. AWS Identity Center provides centralized identity for infrastructure.
Meridian supports automated account provisioning via API and SCIM integration. Customer implements automation workflows through their IdP.
Meridian supports time-limited and temporary account creation with configurable expiration. Customer defines temporary account policies and monitors compliance.

Meridian automatically disables accounts after configurable inactivity thresholds. Customer sets the threshold and reviews disabled accounts. Credential report monitoring flags stale credentials (>90 days unused) for removal.
Account creation, modification, role changes, and deletion are logged automatically in the LMS audit trail. CloudWatch Metric Filters generate alerts on suspicious account activity.

Meridian supports immediate account disabling via admin console and API. Customer identifies high-risk individuals and initiates disabling. GuardDuty triggers automated credential rotation on high-severity findings.

Meridian enforces role-based and attribute-based access control within the LMS. Customer defines organizational access policies and role assignments. Infrastructure access enforced via IAM roles with least-privilege policies.

Meridian enforces application-level data flow controls between domains and user groups. Network-level flow enforced via VPC Security Groups (default-deny, specific allow-lists), NACLs at subnet boundaries, and private subnets for compute/database tiers.

Meridian enforces separation of duties within the LMS (e.g., content author cannot approve own content, admin functions separated from learner functions). Customer defines organizational SoD requirements. Infrastructure changes require PR approval with separate deployer/approver roles.

Meridian implements granular least-privilege role model within the LMS. Customer assigns minimum necessary roles. IAM roles follow least-privilege with no wildcard permissions; legacy IAM users audited and minimized.
Meridian restricts security configuration functions to designated admin roles. Customer authorizes specific personnel for admin access.
Meridian supports separate privileged and standard user accounts. Customer enforces dual-account policy for administrators.
Meridian restricts privileged LMS functions to designated roles. Customer manages who holds privileged roles. Break-glass IAM users require hardware MFA tokens.
All privileged function usage within the LMS is logged with user identity, timestamp, action performed, and source IP. CloudTrail captures infrastructure privileged actions.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Prohibit Non-Privileged Users from Executing Privileged Functions
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Application code enforces role-based restrictions preventing non-privileged users from executing admin functions. No client-side bypass possible.
Configurable account lockout after failed login attempts. Threshold, lockout duration, and reset behavior are application settings.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Meridian LMS supports configurable login banners/system use notifications as a best practice.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Configurable login banner and system use notification displayed before authentication. Customer provides banner text content.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Meridian LMS enforces configurable concurrent session limits as a best practice.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Configurable concurrent session limits per user. New sessions can terminate oldest session or be denied based on configuration.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Endpoint/device control; not applicable to a web-based LMS application.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Endpoint device lock policies managed by customer on their workstations. Not applicable to the LMS web application.
Automatic session termination after configurable inactivity timeout. Session tokens invalidated server-side on timeout or explicit logout.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Permitted Actions Without Identification or Authentication
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: LMS defines which actions are permitted without authentication (e.g., public course catalog if enabled). Public access scope is configurable and disabled by default.
LMS is accessed via HTTPS with TLS 1.2+ enforced on the ALB. Customer configures VPN/Direct Connect for administrative access to infrastructure.
Customer monitors remote access sessions. VPC Flow Logs capture all network traffic. CloudWatch alarms trigger on anomalous access patterns.
Meridian enforces TLS for all application traffic via ACM-managed certificates on ALB and CloudFront. Customer configures encrypted connectivity for infrastructure access.
Network access points managed via VPC architecture. All ingress flows through ALB; compute tier in private subnets with no direct internet access.
Customer restricts privileged remote access. SSM Session Manager replaces SSH with TLS-encrypted, IAM-signed instance access.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Wireless network infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages wireless access in data centers. Not applicable to the LMS application.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Mobile device management; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Customer manages mobile device access policies for their users.
Customer defines policies for accessing the LMS from external systems and personal devices.
Customer defines information sharing policies and authorized recipients for LMS data.
Customer develops organizational security awareness and training policies.
Meridian staff complete security awareness training tracked via GitOps with signed attestations. Customer trains their own personnel on organizational security policies.
Meridian provides insider threat awareness to its staff. Customer provides insider threat training to their personnel.
Meridian staff receive social engineering training with CTF-style challenge tokens proving active engagement. Customer trains their staff on social engineering threats.
Meridian's privileged users complete AWS Skill Builder IAM Deep Dive and role-specific training. Customer provides role-based training to their LMS administrators.
Meridian maintains training records via commit-signed attestation files in version control. Customer maintains their own training records.
Customer develops organizational audit and accountability policies. Meridian documents application audit capabilities.

Meridian generates application-level audit events (login, content access, role changes, admin actions). CloudTrail logs all API activity. Customer defines organizational audit requirements and configures event selection.
LMS audit records include user identity, timestamp, event type, source IP, session ID, outcome, and affected resource.
Audit records include extended metadata: browser agent, domain context, proxy user (if applicable), and request correlation IDs.
Meridian manages application log volume. CloudWatch Logs and S3 provide scalable log storage. Customer configures retention policies and monitors capacity.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Response to Audit Logging Process Failures
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Meridian alerts on application audit logging failures. CloudTrail delivery validated by S3 event notifications. CloudWatch alarms trigger on log delivery failures.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Audit Record Review, Analysis, and Reporting
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Customer reviews and analyzes audit logs. Meridian provides log export capabilities. CloudWatch Metric Filters automate analysis of root logins, unauthorized API calls, security group changes, and failed authentication.
Customer integrates LMS and infrastructure logs into their SIEM for automated correlation and analysis.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Audit Record Reduction and Report Generation
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Meridian provides log search, filtering, and export within the LMS. CloudWatch Logs Insights provides query capability across infrastructure logs.
Meridian uses server clock for audit timestamps. AWS provides synchronized NTP across all infrastructure. Customer ensures client-side time synchronization.
Log integrity protected via S3 Object Lock and CloudTrail Log File Validation (digest hashing). Access to audit logs restricted via S3 bucket policies (security team read-only) and KMS encryption.
Customer defines audit log retention periods. Infrastructure logs retained 365-731 days based on log group configuration.
LMS generates audit records for all security-relevant events. Event types defined in configuration and documented in the Secure Configuration Guide.
Customer develops organizational assessment and authorization policies.
Meridian maintains FedRAMP authorization with automated validation of all 61 KSIs via CI/CD pipeline. Customer conducts organizational assessments.
Customer establishes and manages interconnection security agreements for systems connecting to the LMS.
Meridian tracks application findings with automated remediation tracking. Vulnerability posture monitored via Inspector and SecurityHub. Customer maintains organizational POA&Ms.

Meridian implements continuous monitoring via SecurityHub (CIS/FedRAMP standards), GuardDuty (threat detection), AWS Config (compliance rules), and Inspector (vulnerability scanning). IaC evaluated via Checkov in CI pipeline with drift detection post-deployment. Customer monitors their organizational boundary.
Meridian documents application integration points. VPC Endpoints (S3, DynamoDB) and internal ALB handle internal connections. Customer authorizes system interconnections.
Customer develops organizational CM policies. Meridian documents application CM practices.

Meridian maintains application baseline documented in CodeCommit and served via the config API endpoint. Infrastructure baselines cover EC2 hardening, RDS encryption, S3 policies, and network segmentation. All deployed via Terraform with version-controlled state.

Meridian manages application change control via GitHub PR requirements with separate author/approver. Infrastructure changes require Terraform-only execution with PR approval and immutable audit trails via CloudTrail and CodeCommit commit history.
Meridian performs impact analysis on application changes. Changes validated by Checkov scans in CI pipeline before deployment. AWS Config rules provide continuous drift detection post-deployment.

Meridian restricts application deployment to authorized CI/CD pipeline. Infrastructure changes permitted only via Terraform; no console changes in production. Customer manages IAM permissions for their administrators.
Meridian documents recommended LMS configuration settings in the Secure Configuration Guide (88 security-relevant settings). Customer implements and maintains settings per organizational policy.
Meridian disables unnecessary application features by default. Infrastructure uses immutable containers and minimal AMIs. Customer disables unnecessary services in their environment.
Meridian periodically reviews application functionality. Continuous evaluation of machine-based resources for security improvements via Inspector findings and SSM Patch Manager remediation.

Meridian maintains application component inventory (SBOM). Real-time infrastructure inventory via AWS Config with automatic discovery. SSM Inventory provides OS-level software lists. Customer maintains organizational inventory.
Meridian updates SBOM with each release. AWS Config automatically updates inventory on resource changes.
Customer develops overall CM plan. Meridian provides application CM documentation. Configuration managed via Terraform with state in S3 and DynamoDB locking.
Customer manages software licensing compliance.
Meridian documents where the LMS stores data. Customer tracks data locations across their full environment.
Customer develops organizational contingency planning policies.
Meridian provides application recovery documentation. Recovery plans aligned with RPO=24hrs, RTO=4hrs. Customer develops organizational contingency plan.
Meridian's IR/DR staff complete training including review of incident-response-plan.md and tabletop exercises with tracked attestations. Customer trains their personnel.
Recovery capability tested via quarterly restore operations with duration metrics (current success rate: 92.9%). Customer tests organizational contingency plan.
AWS provides geographically redundant storage. AWS Backup enforces daily backups with 30-day retention.
Multi-AZ ALB and Multi-AZ RDS provide alternate processing. Recovery plan alignment validated via 203 tracked backup jobs.
AWS manages telecommunications redundancy across data centers.

AWS Backup plans enforce daily backups with 30-day retention across RDS, EBS, and S3. Meridian documents application data requiring backup. Customer configures retention policies per regulatory requirements.
Application recovery procedures documented. Multi-AZ architecture supports automated failover. RTO target: 4 hours for full recovery. Customer executes broader organizational recovery plan.
Customer develops organizational I&A policies.

Meridian authenticates users to the LMS via configurable authentication methods (forms, SSO, SAML, PIV/CAC). Customer manages the identity provider. Primary infrastructure authentication via AWS Identity Center.

Meridian supports MFA for privileged accounts including PIV/CAC, TOTP, and email-based OTP. MFA enforced for all human users via AWS Identity Center with FIDO2/WebAuthn. Customer configures and enforces MFA via their IdP.
Meridian supports MFA for all user accounts. Customer enforces MFA policy for non-privileged users through their IdP.
Meridian implements replay-resistant authentication. FIDO2/WebAuthn supported for phishing-resistant authentication. Customer configures IdP for replay resistance.
Customer manages device authentication. Service-to-service authentication uses IAM Roles with no long-lived credentials.
Meridian manages user identifiers within the LMS (unique login IDs with configurable regex restrictions). Customer manages organizational identifiers via directory services.

Meridian enforces configurable password complexity, expiration, history, and minimum length within the LMS. Fallback IAM password policy enforces 14+ characters, complexity, and 90-day rotation. Customer configures policies in their IdP.
Meridian enforces password restrictions including minimum length, special characters, uppercase, numeric requirements, and password history. Customer sets specific thresholds via configuration.
Meridian supports certificate-based authentication. ACM manages TLS certificates with auto-renewal. Customer manages PKI for user certificates.
Authentication feedback is obscured in the LMS UI — passwords masked, generic error messages on failed login to prevent user enumeration.
Meridian supports authentication of external users via configurable authentication methods. Customer defines policies for external user access.
Customer performs identity proofing before provisioning LMS accounts.
Customer develops organizational IR policies. Meridian maintains application-level IR procedures.
Meridian's IR staff complete training including tabletop exercises with attestations tracked in version control. Customer trains their personnel.
Meridian tests IR procedures via tabletop exercises and actual incident metrics review. Customer tests organizational IR plan.

Meridian handles application-level incidents and notifies customer. IR procedures documented in CodeCommit with automated FedRAMP/CISA notification within 1 hour of detection. Playbooks define escalation paths by severity. Customer handles organizational incidents.

Meridian monitors application for incidents via GuardDuty threat detection and SecurityHub findings. Past incidents logged in CloudWatch Logs and S3 audit buckets with pattern analysis. Customer monitors their organizational environment.
Meridian reports application incidents per FedRAMP ICP requirements via automated notification pipeline. Customer reports per organizational and regulatory requirements.
Meridian provides application incident technical support. Customer provides broader organizational IR resources.

Meridian maintains application IR plan with after-action reports generated and stored in version control. Lessons learned incorporated into playbook updates. Customer maintains organizational IR plan.
Customer develops organizational maintenance policies.
Meridian manages application updates and patching. Infrastructure patching via SSM Patch Manager with custom security-critical baselines. Customer manages maintenance windows.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Infrastructure maintenance; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Customer controls remote maintenance access to their environment.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Infrastructure maintenance personnel; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Customer authorizes maintenance personnel.
Customer develops organizational media protection policies.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical media; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages physical media access in data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability: Physical media storage; outside LMS application scope. AWS manages physical media. KSI-SVC-03 applies to logical data encryption (SC-28).
cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical media storage; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages physical media storage and encryption in data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical media sanitization; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS sanitizes media per NIST 800-88.
AWS maintains physical and environmental protection policies for data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical security; outside LMS application scope. Customer (on-prem) or AWS (cloud) responsibility.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages physical access authorizations to data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical security; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages physical access controls at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical security; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS monitors physical access at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical security; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS maintains visitor logs for data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages power equipment protection at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages emergency shutoff at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages emergency power at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages emergency lighting at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages fire protection at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages environmental controls at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: Physical infrastructure; outside LMS application scope.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS manages water damage protection at data centers.
Customer develops organizational planning policies.
Meridian maintains application security plan and FedRAMP authorization package (auto-compiled from CodeCommit governance artifacts). Customer maintains organizational SSP.
Customer develops and enforces rules of behavior for LMS users.

Meridian provides application architecture documentation. Cloud-native architecture uses 21 Lambda functions, managed RDS, API Gateway, with legacy EC2 hardened via IMDSv2 and VPC-only access. Customer develops overall security architecture.
Customer selects NIST baseline. Meridian aligns application controls to FedRAMP Moderate via KSI validation.
Meridian maintains personnel security policies for its staff. Customer maintains policies for their personnel.
Customer designates risk levels for positions with LMS access.
Meridian screens its staff. Customer screens their personnel before granting access.
Meridian disables application accounts upon customer notification. Customer executes termination process and notifies Meridian.
Customer manages role and access changes during personnel transfers.
Customer manages access agreements and NDAs for LMS users.
Customer manages security requirements for contractors and external personnel.
Customer enforces sanctions for security policy violations.
Customer develops organizational risk assessment policies.

cloud_fedramp.applicability_rationale: No KSI mapping; control falls entirely to customer organizational/infrastructure responsibility.
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Meridian conducts application risk assessments. Customer conducts organizational risk assessments.

Meridian performs application SAST/DAST and penetration testing. Infrastructure vulnerability scanning via AWS Inspector with continuous scanning. Findings aggregated into tracked remediation with current posture: 0 CISA KEV matches, 0 internet-reachable criticals. Customer monitors overall vulnerability posture.
Customer responds to identified risks per organizational risk management framework. Security posture assessed by SecurityHub, GuardDuty, Config, and Inspector.
Customer develops organizational SA policies.

Meridian allocates resources for application security with dedicated staff, paid security tools (GuardDuty, SecurityHub, Inspector), and budget tracked in AWS Budgets. Customer allocates organizational resources.
Secure SDLC enforced via GitHub PR requirements, Checkov IaC scanning, and CodeBuild security projects. Aligned with CISA Secure by Design principles.
Customer includes security requirements in procurement. Meridian provides security documentation and FedRAMP authorization evidence.
Meridian provides application security and administration documentation. Customer maintains system-level documentation.
Security engineering principles applied in LMS design: input validation, output encoding, parameterized queries, secure session handling, CSRF protection, and least-privilege defaults.
Customer manages security requirements for external services.
Application source code managed in CodeCommit with PR approval workflows, branch protection, and immutable commit history.
Application security testing includes Checkov scanning in CI, automated vulnerability detection via Inspector, and penetration testing. Vulnerability disclosure policy published at trust center.
Customer develops organizational SC policies.
LMS separates user-facing functions from administrative/management interfaces with distinct role-based access.

Meridian prevents cross-session data leakage within the LMS. Residual data removal automated via S3 Lifecycle Policies and KMS key management for crypto-shredding. AWS provides hypervisor-level isolation.
DDoS protection via AWS Shield Standard (automatic on ALB), WAFv2 Web ACLs, and Route53 anycast. Meridian implements application-level rate limiting and input size controls.

Network boundary enforced via VPC architecture with private subnets for compute/database, Security Group references (no IP-based rules), and ALB as single ingress point. NAT Gateway for egress. Customer manages their organizational network boundaries.
Attack surface minimized via private subnets, Security Group references, and RDS PubliclyAccessible=false. VPC Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB avoid internet transit.
AWS manages external telecom connections at data centers.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Deny by Default / Allow by Exception
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Security Groups implement default-deny with specific allow-lists. NACLs enforce subnet-level deny rules.
All external traffic encrypted via ACM-managed TLS certificates on ALB and CloudFront. TLS 1.2+ enforced with specific SSL policies. Meridian enforces HTTPS for all application communications.
FIPS 140-2 validated modules via AWS GovCloud-compatible services. KMS provides AES-256 encryption. Meridian uses FIPS-validated crypto libraries.
Automatic termination of inactive sessions. Session timeout configurable per deployment.
Cryptographic key management via AWS KMS with automatic annual key rotation. Customer manages key policies. Meridian uses customer-managed keys where applicable.
AES-256 for data at rest (S3, RDS, EBS) via KMS. TLS 1.2+ for data in transit. FIPS 140-2 validated modules.
Certificate management via ACM with auto-renewal. Customer manages PKI lifecycle for user certificates.
Route 53 provides authoritative DNS with DNSSEC support.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Secure Name/Address Resolution Service (Recursive or Caching Resolver)
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: AWS provides secure recursive DNS resolution.

cloud_fedramp.control_name: Architecture and Provisioning for Name/Address Resolution Service
cloud_fedramp.implementation_notes: Route 53 provides highly available, fault-tolerant DNS.

CSRF token validation on all requests, secure cookie attributes (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), and cryptographic session binding. Communication authenticity validated via HTTPS/TLS enforcement and ACM-issued certificates.
Data at rest encrypted via KMS (AES-256) for S3, RDS, and EBS. Customer enables encryption and manages key policies.
AES-256 encryption for all storage via KMS. Customer manages key policies and rotation schedules.
AWS provides hypervisor-level process isolation.
Customer develops organizational SI policies.

Meridian patches application vulnerabilities via secure SDLC pipeline. Infrastructure patching automated via SSM Patch Manager with custom security-critical baselines. Changes validated by Checkov and Config drift detection. Customer applies OS/middleware patches.
Meridian reports application patch status. Inspector provides continuous vulnerability assessment with automated findings tracking.
Immutable infrastructure via Terraform prevents unauthorized modification. Trusted base images only. Customer manages endpoint antimalware.

GuardDuty provides continuous threat detection. SecurityHub aggregates findings against CIS/FedRAMP standards. CloudWatch alarms on failed logins, log delivery failures, and security group changes. Customer configures additional monitoring per organizational needs.

Meridian issues LMS security advisories. Third-party software vulnerabilities monitored via Inspector with continuous scanning. AWS publishes infrastructure advisories. Customer monitors and responds.
Meridian provides signed releases. CloudTrail Log File Validation provides digest hashing of audit trails. S3 bucket versioning on audit/artifact storage for tamper prevention.
Input validation and sanitization enforced throughout the LMS: parameterized queries, output encoding, file upload validation with content-type sniffing, and configurable allowed file types.
Secure error handling without information disclosure. Generic error messages prevent enumeration. Detailed errors logged server-side only.
Customer defines data retention and disposal policies. Data removal capabilities include S3 Lifecycle Policies and KMS crypto-shredding for encrypted backups.
Meridian applies secure coding practices. AWS provides infrastructure-level memory protections.
Customer develops organizational supply chain risk management policies.
Meridian maintains supply chain risk management for LMS dependencies in version-controlled policy documents. Customer maintains organizational SCRM plan.
Meridian implements controls for LMS dependencies with formal approval rule templates and version-controlled audit trails. Customer implements organizational supply chain controls.
Customer implements secure acquisition strategies.
Customer assesses and reviews suppliers.
Customer establishes notification agreements with suppliers.
Customer inspects systems upon delivery.
Meridian provides SBOM and signed releases. Third-party vulnerabilities monitored via Inspector with continuous scanning for OS and application components. Customer verifies authenticity.

ksi_reference.description: Apply FedRAMP Minimum Assessment Scope
Address all FedRAMP 20x KSI requirements and recommendations
Ensure all employees receive security and privacy awareness training
Educate employees on recognizing and responding to supply chain and social engineering threats
Role-specific training for dev/engineering staff on secure software practices
Log and monitor service modifications
Automate configuration management and enforce approved baselines
Implement persistent automated testing and validation of changes
Consistently follow a documented change management procedure
Test changes in non-production environments before deployment
Configure all machine-based information resources to limit inbound/outbound traffic
Enforce encrypted and authenticated communications
Implement secure session management and network termination
Use trusted and verified base images/containers
Protect against denial of service attacks and unwanted spam
Retired in Phase Two
Use immutable, segmented, cloud-native architectures
Use automated services to persistently assess security posture (Moderate only)
Enforce phishing-resistant MFA for all user authentication
Implement passwordless or strong passwords with MFA
Authenticate and authorize machine-to-machine communications
Use least-privileged, role/attribute-based, just-in-time authorization
Design IAM systems assuming resources will be compromised (zero trust)
Disable inactive/unnecessary accounts and respond to suspicious activity
Securely manage lifecycle and privileges of all accounts, roles, and groups
Respond to incidents per FedRAMP requirements and provider policies
Review past incidents for patterns; generate after-action reports
Incorporate lessons learned into operations
Operate SIEM or similar for centralized, tamper-resistant logging
Regularly review and audit logs
Rapidly detect and respond to vulnerabilities per FedRAMP VRD standard
Retired — superseded by KSI-MLA-03
Perform IaC and configuration evaluation and testing
Retired — superseded by KSI-MLA-03
Maintain list of monitored/logged/audited resources and event types
Use least-privileged, just-in-time access for log data (Moderate only)
Generate inventories of information resources from authoritative sources
Document security objectives and requirements for each information resource
Perform penetration testing
Build security and privacy into SDLC; align with CISA Secure By Design
Reserved / Placeholder
Have staff and budget for security commensurate with risk
Manage and mitigate technical debt
Plan and maintain system resilience and recovery capabilities
Maintain alternate processing/storage for disaster recovery
Perform and protect system backups
Regularly test contingency and recovery plans
Continuously evaluate machine-based resources for security improvements
Follow FedRAMP encryption policies for data in transit
Encrypt information at rest by default
Manage configuration of machine-based resources using automation
Use cryptographic methods to validate integrity of machine-based resources
Manage cryptographic key lifecycle
Continuously assess security posture and risk
Ensure changes do not leave residual security issues (Moderate only)
Continuously validate authenticity/integrity of resource communications (Moderate only)
Remove unwanted information promptly (Moderate only)
Follow FedRAMP MAS requirements for third-party information resources
Assess and manage third-party security requirements
Identify and prioritize mitigation of supply chain risks
Monitor third-party software for upstream vulnerabilities
Determine how authorization data will be shared in alignment with ADS standard
ksi_reference.nist_controls: AC-3, AC-4, AU-2, AU-3, AU-6, CA-2, IR-4, RA-5, SC-8

ksi_reference.description: Document vulnerability detection and response methodology
Verify SCN procedures are documented and active change tracking maintained
Maintain plan for Ongoing Authorization Reports and Quarterly Reviews
Document the secure configuration baseline for the cloud service offering
Operate a secure inbox for FedRAMP and government communications
Persistently validate security posture using automated pipelines
Integrate FedRAMP ICP into incident response procedures
Ensure cryptographic modules align with FedRAMP UCM policy
Role-specific training for IR/DR staff
Persistently review executive support for security objectives
